GPUscale.net

v4.7
LLM capacity & dimensioning studio: model, precision, workload and hardware in; memory fit, latency, throughput and resilient topology out.
100% local: nothing you enter is saved or uploaded
Inputs · work top to bottom, results update live
Model
Architecture geometry drives every estimate: total params size the weights, active params drive compute and decode reads, and the effective KV heads × head dim encode each model's real cache cost (MLA, sparse and SSM hybrids use sheet-compatible effective values).
B
B
tokens
Precision
Bytes per weight. FP8 is the 2026 serving default (half of BF16, minimal loss). NVFP4/MXFP4 are Blackwell-native 4-bit floats; Q-tiers are GGUF/llama.cpp formats.Typical production: FP8 · edge/workstation: Q4_K_M or MXFP4
Bytes per KV element. FP8 KV halves cache cost with negligible quality impact and is the vLLM production default.Typical: FP8 · long-context on tight VRAM: INT4
Workload
Presets fill resident sequence, reasoning mode, visible output and the three SLO targets with realistic values for the workload. Everything stays editable afterwards.
Thinking tokens generated before the visible answer. They add decode time and, if the toggle below is on, extend the KV footprint too.Light ≈ 2,000 tok · Heavy ≈ 8,000 tok
On for standard serving (thinking stays in context). Off if the engine discards reasoning traces from the cache after each call.
Running batch: only admitted sequences hold KV and the rest queue (the modern default). All sessions: every concurrent call keeps its cache resident (lowest latency variance, far more memory).
Pass/fail thresholds for the compliance panel. A target of 0 disables that check. Presets refill these with workload-appropriate values.
ms
tok/s
s
Concurrency estimator · Little's law
→ 0 concurrent calls
Hardware & resilience
Bandwidth sets decode speed, dense FP16 Tensor-Core TFLOPS sets prefill speed, VRAM sets what fits. 2026 parts (VR200, MI455X) carry pre-launch estimates.
Pick what the deployment must survive. The sizing rule: every figure in the results is what you can guarantee even during the covered failure, so throughput and concurrency never grow with resilience. The extra hardware either idles until a failure (spares, mirrors, DR standby) or serves day-to-day as burst headroom (Active/Active). The economics strip under the topology shows guaranteed vs normal-day capacity, idle hardware and the cost multiplier for the chosen model.
%
How full Auto-size may pack the serving GPUs. 80% leaves industry-standard growth headroom; 90 to 95% packs tighter at the cost of burst and growth margin (the thin-headroom warning starts at 92%). Manual sliders are never limited by this.
Uses everything above: model, precision, workload, SLO targets, GPU and node size. Picks the smallest Tensor parallel that fits one copy of the model (widened if the TTFT target demands it), then the fewest workers and the batch that admit the peak concurrent calls. No SLO targets means offline: minimal hardware, largest fitting batch, queueing allowed.
Fine-tune · Auto-size sets these, edit freely
Advanced tuning
Efficiency assumptions · defaults are sensible
Results · computed from the inputs on the left
—
In plain words
Memory ledger
Time to first token
0ms
Per-user speed
0tok/s
Aggregate throughput
0tok/s
Mean user latency
0s
SLO compliance
Deployment topology · workers & GPU utilization
Resilience buys survival, not speed: the guaranteed figures are what remains during the failure you chose to cover. Idle spares add nothing day-to-day; active/active sites add burst headroom you should not plan sustained load on.
Throughput vs admitted sequencesbatch-aware
per-user tok/s aggregate tok/s memory limit operating point
Per-user speed vs context lengthat current batch
per-user tok/s current context
Request latency anatomy
Reading of this configuration
Recommendations
GPUscale.net

Capacity & dimensioning studio for self-hosted LLM deployments. All figures are peak estimates; production typically achieves 70–90%. Validate with vLLM bench / GenAI-Perf before commitments.

© 2026 GPUscale.net · Released under the MIT License

Project

github.com/mahmoudyassine/gpu-scale-tool Releases & changelog Report an issue Portable single-file build

Studio

Version — Engine — · library — Scripting API at window.GPUscale Private by design: everything runs in your browser. Nothing you enter is saved, uploaded or tracked. No accounts, no analytics, no cookies.
